notes.note: Caesar's Anticato, a pamphlet attacking the memory of Cato the Younger written in answer to Cicero's own laudatory Cato. Cicero, for political safety, sends Caesar a letter praising it, vetted first by Caesar's agents Oppius and Balbus.
C. Oppius and the elder L. Cornelius Balbus, Caesar's two chief political managers at Rome, who screen Cicero's letter before it goes forward.

Mancipatio, the solemn Roman ceremony for transferring ownership of valuable property, requiring scales, a bronze ingot, and five adult witnesses. A slave is handed over by this rite as the legal mechanism for conveying Cicero's share of the estate.
The estate of the deceased Brinnius, of which Cicero was a co-heir; its sale and conveyance at Puteoli, handled through the agent Vestorius, recur across the late 45 BC letters.
Hetereius, the buyer to whom Cicero's share of the Brinnian estate is to be conveyed at Puteoli; known only from this transaction.
Caesar's expected progress through Campania after his return from Spain in 45 BC, which Cicero and his neighbours anxiously track; it culminates in the famous visit narrated in 13.52.
A coastal town of Etruria northwest of Rome on the Via Aurelia, where Cicero plans to wait to meet Caesar; he is arranging borrowed lodgings nearby.
